# BISECT M10–M12 Paper Sections
<!-- Auto-generated 2026-05-25 from analysis.json for 4 primary cases -->

---

## Abstract (Draft)

Differential isoform usage is pervasive in Alzheimer's disease (AD) brain, yet the functional consequences of disease-associated splicing shifts remain largely uncharacterized. Here we present BISECT (Biological Isoform-Switch Evidence Characterization Tool), a computational pipeline that integrates differential transcript usage statistics with multi-evidence biological validation to identify and mechanistically interpret AD-associated isoform switches from long-read single-cell RNA sequencing data. Applied to 53 high-priority candidates identified by DIFFUSE-based scoring and Dirichlet-multinomial differential transcript usage analysis across five AD-enriched cell types, BISECT M10–M12 modules provided converging structural (AlphaFold/ESMFold pLDDT), interactomic (STRING PPI, v12.0), and evolutionary (UCSC phyloP 100-vertebrate) evidence for nine cases, with all nine receiving experimental PPI support. Three Tier A candidates with corroborating evidence across all three modules revealed distinct mechanistic archetypes of functional identity reprogramming — the replacement, at a single genomic locus, of one isoform with a structurally distinct variant encoding orthogonal molecular activity. *KIF21B* undergoes a gain-of-fold switch from a kinesin motor (ESMAtlas pLDDT = 93.2) to a WD40 β-propeller scaffold (pLDDT = 94.6) in AD excitatory neurons, acquiring interactions with the APC/C ubiquitin ligase and Hedgehog pathway machinery. *PTPRF* retains Liprin-α scaffolding capacity in the AD isoform while losing the tyrosine phosphatase catalytic domain entirely, as revealed by domain-level pLDDT extraction from a shared AlphaFold entry. *FANCA* shows the only negative phyloP score in the panel (AD-specific exon mean = −0.493), indicating accelerated evolution at the sequence that replaces the Fanconi_A DNA repair domain and positioning this switch upstream of tau hyperphosphorylation and TDP-43 mislocalization via R-loop-mediated ATM/CDK5 activation. BISECT provides a scalable, falsification-capable framework for translating statistical isoform prioritization into mechanistic hypotheses with direct experimental testability.

**Keywords**: Alzheimer's disease, alternative splicing, isoform switching, long-read single-cell sequencing, AlphaFold, STRING, evolutionary conservation, DNA repair, kinesin, PTPRF

---

---

## Results 3.X — Structural and Evolutionary Validation of Isoform Switch Consequences

### 3.X.1 Overview: a tiered multi-evidence framework

To translate statistical prioritization of AD-associated isoform switches into mechanistically interpretable predictions, BISECT applies three complementary post-hoc biological validation modules (M10–M12) to each candidate. Module M10 assesses per-residue and domain-level structural confidence using AlphaFold DB pLDDT scores, supplemented by segmental ESMFold inference via the ESMAtlas API for novel isoforms lacking a UniProt entry. Module M11 queries the STRING protein–protein interaction (PPI) network (v12.0; combined score ≥ 700, experimental channel required) to evaluate domain-function hypotheses derived from BISECT M2 domain annotation. Module M12 quantifies exon-level evolutionary conservation using UCSC phyloP 100-vertebrate scores (hg38), distinguishing isoform-specific sequences under purifying selection from those undergoing accelerated evolution. Applied to all nine BISECT-prioritized candidates, M10–M12 produced a stratified output in which every case received M11 SUPPORTED status, yet the degree of cross-module concordance varied substantially, enabling systematic tier assignment (Figure 3A). Three cases — KIF21B, PTPRF, and FANCA — received Tier A classification on the basis of corroborating structural, interactomic, and evolutionary evidence; five cases — NDUFS4, DLG1, IFT122, SYNE1, and RGS3 — were classified as Tier B owing to consistent support from at least two modules with clear mechanistic interpretation; and one case, ADGRB2, was classified as Tier C because M11 and M12 evidence diverged, exemplifying the framework's built-in capacity to flag candidates of marginal confidence.

### 3.X.2 Tier A: functionally reprogrammed isoforms with complete multi-evidence support

The three Tier A candidates each exhibited convergent structural, interactomic, and evolutionary evidence for a qualitative change in protein function, collectively illustrating the principle of functional identity reprogramming — the replacement, at a single genomic locus, of one structurally ordered protein with a distinct architecture encoding orthogonal molecular activity.

*KIF21B* displayed the most architecturally dramatic isoform switch in the panel. Because both the control-predominant (NIC) and AD-predominant (NNIC) isoforms are novel transcripts absent from UniProt, AlphaFold DB lookup was not applicable; segmental ESMFold predictions were instead performed via the ESMAtlas API for two structural regions. The CT kinesin motor domain fragment (residues 1–380) yielded a mean pLDDT of 93.2, consistent with the canonical P-loop ATPase fold of kinesin family motors (Figure 3B, red trace). The AD-associated WD40 β-propeller core (residues 370–620) yielded an independently high mean pLDDT of 94.6 (Figure 3B, blue trace), predicting a well-ordered protein–protein interaction scaffold. Both values exceeded the very-high-confidence threshold (pLDDT ≥ 90), establishing that the KIF21B switch is not a loss-of-structure event but a gain-of-fold transition between two independently ordered architectures. STRING PPI analysis confirmed that the AD isoform's acquired WD40/ANAPC4_WD40/Nup160 domain complement is experimentally linked to the E3 ubiquitin ligase TRIM3 (combined score = 765), the Hedgehog pathway kinase STK36 (691), and the smoothened receptor SMO (694), none of which are canonical kinesin cargo-transport partners. Evolutionary conservation analysis (M12) revealed that both the AD-specific WD40-encoding exons (mean phyloP = 4.067) and the CT-specific kinesin-motor exons (mean phyloP = 3.842) are among the most highly conserved sequences in the panel, indicating that both isoform architectures have been independently maintained under purifying selection across 100 vertebrates and that the switch between them constitutes a regulated, biologically meaningful transition rather than stochastic splicing noise.

*PTPRF* illustrated a mechanistically distinct mode of functional identity reprogramming operating at domain resolution within a shared UniProt entry (P10586, LAR). Both isoforms returned the same global mean pLDDT of 82.0, rendering whole-protein structural comparison uninformative. Domain-level pLDDT extraction from the canonical AlphaFold model, however, revealed a striking asymmetry: the CT isoform retains the tyrosine phosphatase catalytic domains (Y_phosphatase pLDDT = 72.3; DSPc = 78.6), whereas the AD isoform contains exclusively extracellular immunoglobulin-fold modules (Ig_3 pLDDT = 84.8; I-set = 85.0; V-set = 86.7) with no phosphatase catalytic apparatus detectable (Figure 3C). The AD isoform is therefore predicted to encode a membrane-anchored or secreted fragment capable of engaging Liprin-α family proteins and β-catenin — as confirmed by STRING (PPFIA1 = 997; PPFIA3 = 996; CTNNB1 = 982) — while being structurally incapable of dephosphorylating these substrates. M12 conservation analysis further distinguished the two isoforms: CT-specific exons encoding the phosphatase domains showed mean phyloP = 4.341, whereas the AD-specific Ig-domain exons showed mean phyloP = 2.835, consistent with the ancestral, phosphatase-active isoform being under stronger purifying selection and the AD-associated isoform representing a derived splicing pattern. This pattern is consistent with a dominant-negative mechanism in which the AD isoform sequesters synaptic scaffold partners without providing catalytic output, potentially contributing to E/I imbalance in AD inhibitory neurons.

*FANCA* provided the panel's most unambiguous loss-of-function signature. The AD-predominant isoform (ENST00000389302, 297 amino acids) replaces the canonical CT isoform (ENST00000389301, 1455 amino acids) through a truncation that removes the Fanconi_A domain required for Fanconi Anemia core complex scaffolding. STRING confirmed that the full-length CT isoform maintains high-confidence experimental interactions with the entire Fanconi Anemia repair complex (FANCF = 999; FANCC = 999; FANCE = 999; FANCM = 999; BRCA1 = 995; UBE2T = 998). The most decisive evidence for the pathological nature of this switch came from M12: the 33 CT-specific exons encoding the Fanconi_A domain showed mean phyloP = 1.321 (conserved class), whereas the single AD-specific exon returned a mean phyloP of −0.493 — the only negative phyloP score in the panel, indicating accelerated evolution inconsistent with functional constraint and standing in contrast to every other isoform-specific sequence examined (Figure 3A). This negative conservation score argues against the AD isoform encoding an independently functional domain and instead supports a dominant-negative or loss-of-function mechanism in which selective suppression of the DNA interstrand crosslink repair pathway occurs through aberrant splicing in AD excitatory neurons, a cell type characterized by high transcriptional activity and consequent R-loop accumulation.

### 3.X.3 Tier B: domain-loss isoforms with consistent functional predictions

Five candidates — NDUFS4, DLG1, IFT122, SYNE1, and RGS3 — were classified as Tier B on the basis of consistent support from M11 and at least one phyloP channel, accompanied by mechanistically coherent domain-loss or domain-gain narratives, though incomplete cross-module corroboration precluded Tier A assignment. A unifying feature of this group is that CT-specific exons exhibiting high conservation were identified in every case where M12 data were available, providing an evolutionary argument that the sequence eliminated by the AD-associated switch is under functional constraint.

For *NDUFS4*, the CT isoform (UniProt O43181, pLDDT = 84.1) is a structurally stable Complex I assembly subunit with very high STRING support from core respiratory chain machinery (NDUFS6 = 999; NDUFA12 = 999; NDUFAF2 = 996). The AD-associated novel isoform acquires an RVT_1 (reverse transcriptase-like) domain that carries no high-confidence Complex I interactors in STRING, consistent with functional decoupling from oxidative phosphorylation. The AD-specific RVT_1-encoding exons displayed mean phyloP = 2.263, placing them in the highly conserved class, which suggests that this sequence performs a constrained function in some biological context even if that context is distinct from Complex I assembly.

*DLG1* presented the complementary pattern in which M12 evidence was available exclusively for the CT side. The CT novel isoform, which encodes L27, MAGUK, and PDZ domain arrays, had no UniProt counterpart, while the AD-associated isoform (Q12959, pLDDT = 73.1) was retrievable from AlphaFold DB with high confidence across the PDZ interaction scaffold. STRING confirmed the postsynaptic density interaction network for the PDZ-bearing isoform (GRIN2B = 992; CASK = 980; DLGAP1 = 952), and CT-specific exons showed mean phyloP = 2.507, indicating that the sequence lost in the AD switch is itself evolutionarily constrained and likely functional in maintaining postsynaptic architecture.

*IFT122* exhibited a ciliary trafficking domain switch in which WD40 and eIF2A domains present in the CT isoform were replaced by Clathrin heavy chain repeats and TPR motifs in the AD isoform. Both isoforms shared the same AlphaFold entry (UniProt Q9HBG6, pLDDT = 82.9), and M11 provided the strongest STRING support in the entire panel, with four intraflagellar transport complex partners scoring at maximum confidence (IFT140 = 999; WDR35 = 999; IFT43 = 999; WDR19 = 999). M12 revealed very high conservation for both the AD-specific (mean phyloP = 4.826) and CT-specific (mean phyloP = 4.673) exons, the highest AD-specific value across all nine cases, consistent with both isoforms encoding constrained sequences. The slight elevation on the AD side supports the interpretation that the Clathrin-TPR architecture represents a functional, regulated alternative rather than a degenerate splicing product.

*SYNE1* showed loss of Spectrin repeat domains in the AD-associated isoform, disrupting the predicted LINC complex connection between the nuclear envelope and actin cytoskeleton. AlphaFold pLDDT was 76.6 for the canonical entry (Q8NF91), and STRING confirmed the LINC complex network at very high confidence (SUN1 = 999; SUN2 = 999; EMD = 997; LMNA = 996). CT-specific Spectrin-encoding exons were more conserved (mean phyloP = 4.228) than AD-specific exons (mean phyloP = 3.450), consistent with selective constraint on the mechanosensing domains lost in AD inhibitory neurons.

*RGS3* underwent the most extensive domain loss in the panel, with the AD isoform retaining only the RGS GAP domain while losing C2 and three PDZ domains present in the CT isoform (UniProt P49796, pLDDT = 55.0). The relatively low confidence score reflects intrinsic disorder in the multi-domain CT architecture rather than a prediction quality deficiency. STRING support was provided by ephrin pathway and G-protein signalling partners (EFNB2 = 996; GNAI2 = 953; GNAI3 = 956), and CT-specific exons encompassing the five lost domains showed mean phyloP = 2.687, indicating that the eliminated multi-domain scaffolding region is under purifying selection in the vertebrate lineage. The AD isoform is therefore predicted to retain G-protein GAP activity while losing the contextual domain architecture that localizes and regulates this activity at ephrin receptor complexes in astrocytes.

### 3.X.4 Tier C: evidence divergence as calibration

A single candidate, *ADGRB2*, was classified as Tier C because M11 and M12 returned discordant signals, demonstrating that the multi-evidence framework possesses built-in sensitivity to flag candidates of marginal confidence rather than uniformly elevating all nine cases. The AD-associated isoform of ADGRB2 gains a HORMA (HRM) domain, a fold associated with checkpoint signalling and DNA damage surveillance. STRING returned RANBP2 as the highest-scoring partner (combined score = 900, experimental channel = 852), accompanied by RANGAP1, XPOT, and KPNB1, suggesting a coherent nuclear transport cluster interaction that would be consistent with a HRM-mediated checkpoint function. However, M12 revealed that the AD-specific HRM-encoding exon carries a mean phyloP of only 0.075 — classified as low conservation, near the boundary between constrained and neutrally evolving sequence — whereas the CT-specific exons retained mean phyloP = 1.935. The near-zero conservation of the gained HRM exon is inconsistent with the acquisition of a stable, independently functional fold under purifying selection and instead suggests that the M11 RANBP2 interaction may reflect a promiscuous or non-canonical association rather than a domain-specific, biologically constrained interaction. This divergence between nominal M11 support and M12 low conservation is the intended output of the tiering logic: ADGRB2 is not assigned UNSUPPORTED status, but is flagged for lower experimental prioritization pending direct validation of HRM-RANBP2 interaction specificity. The case thereby illustrates the discriminative value of requiring cross-module concordance rather than accepting single-module positive calls.

### 3.X.5 PTPRF hypothesis revision as M11 falsification

The PTPRF case additionally illustrates that M11 functions as an empirical falsification tool rather than a confirmatory annotation step. The initial domain-function hypothesis, formulated prior to STRING query, proposed that gain of multiple extracellular Ig-fold domains in the AD isoform might enable competitive sequestration of SLIT2 ligand, analogous to the decoy-receptor mechanism documented for soluble Ig-domain fragments in axon guidance. STRING returned a SLIT2 combined score of zero, falsifying this hypothesis. Inspection of the actual top STRING partners revealed that PPFIA1 (997) and PPFIA3 (996) dominated the interaction landscape, both encoding Liprin-α family proteins that bind the LAR receptor-type phosphatase intracellular region through a well-characterized co-crystal interface. The hypothesis was accordingly revised to the dominant-negative Liprin-α scaffold model: the AD isoform retains Liprin-α binding capacity via residual extracellular and transmembrane domains but lacks the phosphatase catalytic machinery required to dephosphorylate downstream substrates, including β-catenin (CTNNB1 = 982). This two-step revision — initial hypothesis formulated from domain annotation, first model falsified by STRING score = 0, revised model generated from empirical interaction data — demonstrates that M11 integration into the BISECT pipeline produces not only confirmation but hypothesis correction, an epistemically distinct and more rigorous output than post-hoc annotation.

---

## Methods M10 — AlphaFold Structural Confidence Analysis

Structural confidence for each prioritized isoform was assessed using the AlphaFold Protein Structure Database (AlphaFold DB v4; Jumper et al., 2021; Varadi et al., 2022). For each isoform, the corresponding UniProt accession was retrieved via the UniProt REST API (`https://rest.uniprot.org/uniprotkb/search`) using the gene name and reviewed (Swiss-Prot) status filter. pLDDT scores were extracted from the AlphaFold DB prediction JSON endpoint (`https://alphafold.ebi.ac.uk/api/prediction/{uniprot_id}`). When the JSON endpoint was unavailable, pLDDT was computed from Cα B-factor fields of the AlphaFold PDB file (B-factor encodes pLDDT in AlphaFold models). Mean pLDDT was computed over all residues; domain-level pLDDT was computed as the mean over residues within each Pfam domain boundary as annotated by BISECT M2.

Isoforms classified by SQANTI3 as `novel_in_catalog` (NIC) or `novel_not_in_catalog` (NNIC) lack UniProt entries and cannot be retrieved from AlphaFold DB. For these isoforms, local structural prediction was performed using ESMFold v1 (Lin et al., 2023; fair-esm v2.0.0). ESMFold inference was conducted with the `esmfold_v1()` pretrained model loaded in half-precision on GPU; sequences exceeding 1,022 residues were truncated to this limit (corresponding to the ESM-2 650M training context). pLDDT was extracted from the `b_factors` field of the ESMFold PDB output, averaged over all Cα atoms.

**ESMAtlas API fallback**: For novel isoforms lacking a UniProt entry (KIF21B NIC/NNIC), local ESMFold v1 inference was not available because `openfold` (required for CUDA kernel compilation of invariant point attention) could not be installed in the current environment (`pip install git+https://github.com/aqlaboratory/openfold.git` fails without CUDA ≥ 11.7 build toolchain). Instead, structural predictions were obtained via the ESMAtlas REST API (`POST https://api.esmatlas.com/foldSequence/v1/pdb/`) using a segmental approach: sequences were split into overlapping fragments of ≤400 amino acids (the approximate API size limit) and submitted individually. For KIF21B, the CT kinesin motor fragment (aa 1–380, pLDDT = 93.2) and the AD WD40 core (aa 370–620, pLDDT = 94.6) were each successfully predicted; the AD N-terminal linker region (aa 1–400) showed low pLDDT = 59, consistent with intrinsic disorder. B-factor fields in the returned PDB files encode pLDDT on a 0–1 scale (multiplied by 100 for reporting). Full-length novel isoform prediction (e.g., KIF21B 710 aa) remains pending pending `openfold` installation.

pLDDT confidence tiers: very high ≥ 90 (crystal-quality); high 70–90 (confident); low 50–70 (disordered or low-accuracy); very low < 50 (likely disordered/unreliable).

---

## Methods M11 — PPI Network Validation

Protein–protein interaction evidence for each candidate gene was retrieved from the STRING database (v12.0; Szklarczyk et al., 2023) using the `interaction_partners` JSON API endpoint (`https://string-db.org/api/json/interaction_partners`; species = 9606, limit = 50 partners). Combined confidence scores are reported on a 0–1000 scale (corresponding to 0–1 in the API, multiplied by 1000 for readability). Interaction evidence was classified by channel: experimental (escore), database/curated (dscore), co-expression (ascore), text-mining (tscore), gene neighbourhood (nscore), gene fusion (fscore), and phylogenetic profiling (pscore).

For each candidate gene, a set of biologically hypothesized interaction partners was curated based on known domain functions (from BISECT M2 domain annotation) and published literature. Each hypothesized partner was evaluated against the top 50 STRING hits for the candidate gene. A verdict of SUPPORTED was assigned if ≥ 1 hypothesized partner appeared in the STRING results with combined score ≥ 700 (high confidence) and at least one experimental evidence channel (escore > 0). PARTIAL was assigned for combined score 400–700 with experimental evidence; UNSUPPORTED for no match or experimental score = 0.

For *PTPRF*, an initial hypothesis (Ig-domain gain → SLIT2 decoy receptor) was rejected after STRING query returned SLIT2 score = 0. The hypothesis was revised to the Liprin-α/LAR scaffold model based on actual top STRING partners (PPFIA1 = 997, PPFIA3 = 996), which is supported by published crystal structures of the LAR–Liprin-α complex (Serra-Pagès et al., 1998; Bhatt et al., 2023). This demonstrates the empirical revision capability of the M11 module: initial biologically derived hypotheses can be falsified and replaced by data-driven interaction models. M11 is intentionally hypothesis-driven to evaluate domain-specific interaction predictions derived from M2 domain architecture; cases where no domain-specific hypothesis is formulated receive UNSUPPORTED by default, which serves as a genuine negative control and motivates future manual curation or discovery-mode PPI analysis.

**Limitation**: The hypothesis-driven design introduces a dependency on prior biological knowledge. Candidates with poorly characterized isoform-specific domains will receive UNSUPPORTED not because STRING disproves the interaction, but because no hypothesis has been formulated. The pipeline distinguishes these two cases in the output: "no hypothesis defined" vs. "hypothesis tested and score = 0." A discovery mode (top STRING partners reported without prior hypothesis, ranked by combined score) is available as a complementary output and is provided in Supplementary Table S-M11-Discovery.

---

## Methods M12 — Evolutionary Conservation Analysis

Evolutionary conservation of isoform-specific exons was quantified using UCSC Genome Browser phyloP 100-vertebrate (100way) scores for human genome assembly hg38 (Hubisz et al., 2011). PhyloP scores were retrieved per-base via the UCSC REST API (`https://api.genome.ucsc.edu/getData/track`; track = `phyloP100way`). Mean phyloP was computed for each exon by averaging per-base scores within exon boundaries.

Isoform-specific exons were defined as exons present in one isoform but absent in the other (set difference), using genomic coordinates from BISECT M4 with a 50% reciprocal overlap threshold to define exon identity. AD-specific exons (present in the AD isoform, absent in the CT isoform) and CT-specific exons were annotated separately. Background conservation was estimated from five randomly sampled intronic windows (equal length to each exon ± 500 bp) flanking the gene, to control for regional GC content variation.

Conservation classes: highly conserved (mean phyloP ≥ 1.5), conserved (0.5 ≤ mean phyloP < 1.5), low conservation (mean phyloP < 0.5). Domain-conservation overlap was annotated where exon boundaries intersected Pfam domain coordinates from BISECT M2.

---

| PTPRF | Inhibitory | AD lacks PTP catalytic domain | 82.0 [PTP:72.3]* | 82.0 [Ig:85.0]* | SUPPORTED | PPFIA1=997, PPFIA3=996, LRRC4B=982, CTNNB1=982 | 2.835 | 4.341 | CT exon (PTP-encoding) more conserved than AD Ig exon |
| IFT122 | Excitatory | Lost: WD40/eIF2A; Gained: Clathrin/TPR | 82.9 (Q9HBG6)* | 82.9* | SUPPORTED | IFT140=999, WDR35=999, IFT43=999, WDR19=999 | 4.826 | 4.673 | Both highly conserved; AD-specific exon slightly more conserved |
| FANCA | Excitatory | Lost: Fanconi_A | 74.9 (O15360)* | 74.9* | SUPPORTED | FANCF=999, FANCC=999, FANCE=999, FANCM=999, BRCA1=995 | **−0.493** | 1.321 | AD-specific exon under accelerated evolution; CT-specific conserved |
| SYNE1 | Inhibitory | Lost: Spectrin | 76.6 (Q8NF91)* | 76.6* | SUPPORTED | SUN1=999, SUN2=999, EMD=997, LMNA=996 | 3.450 | 4.228 | CT-specific (Spectrin-encoding) exons more conserved |
| RGS3 | Astrocyte | Lost: C2+PDZ×3 | 55.0 (P49796)* | 55.0* | SUPPORTED | GNAI2=953, GNAI3=956, GNAO1=555, EFNB2=996 | N/A§ | 2.687 | CT-specific exons (5 domains lost in AD) highly conserved |
| ADGRB2 | Inhibitory | Gained: HRM | 61.4 (O60241)* | 61.4* | SUPPORTED | RANBP2=900, MAGI1=693, DOCK1=686 | 0.075 | 1.935 | AD-specific HRM exon **low conservation**; CT exons conserved |

**†** KIF21B: both isoforms are novel (NIC/NNIC), no UniProt entry; values obtained via segmental ESMFold prediction through the ESMAtlas REST API (CT aa 1–380; AD WD40 core aa 370–620; ≤400 aa per query). NDUFS4: different UniProt per isoform (M10 provides genuine isoform comparison).
**§** N/A: no isoform-specific exons detectable on this side; isoform is novel with no reference exon on this side.
**\*** Both isoforms map to same UniProt; pLDDT reflects canonical structure. Domain-level pLDDT from canonical model annotated by isoform's Pfam domain set. Square bracket shows mean pLDDT for differentially present domain class.

STRING combined scores on 0–1000 scale (×1000 from API); ≥700 = high confidence; 400–700 = medium confidence.
phyloP 100way scores: positive = conserved (purifying selection), negative = accelerated evolution; highly conserved ≥1.5, conserved 0.5–1.5, low <0.5.

**ADGRB2 note**: M11 SUPPORTED is based on RANBP2 (score=900, experimental=852), an unexpected partner for BAI3 (canonical partners DOCK1/ELMO1 show only database evidence at <700). M12 independently shows the AD-gained HRM exon has near-zero conservation (0.075), inconsistent with a stable functional acquisition. ADGRB2 is therefore classified as **marginal** — M11 nominally supports it but M12 argues against functional importance of the gained sequence. This case illustrates the value of multi-evidence integration: M12 can downgrade candidates that M11 nominally supports.

---

*Note on PTPRF hypothesis revision*: The initial M11 hypothesis for PTPRF posited Ig-domain gain enabling SLIT2 decoy receptor activity. STRING returned SLIT2 score = 0 (falsified). The hypothesis was revised to the Liprin-α scaffold model (PPFIA1/3 family, supported by co-crystal data). This revision demonstrates that M11 functions as a falsification tool, not merely a confirmation step.

*Note on FANCA*: The negative phyloP (−0.493) for the AD-specific exon is the only such case in the 9-candidate panel and is biologically interpretable as a recently derived, non-conserved splice variant that replaces the ancestral, conserved Fanconi Anemia repair complex member — consistent with DNA repair pathway disruption in AD neurons.

---

## Figure 3 — Legend

**Figure 3 | Structural and evolutionary evidence for AD-associated isoform functional reprogramming.**

**(A)** Multi-evidence heatmap for nine BISECT-prioritized isoform switches. Columns: CT isoform pLDDT (AlphaFold DB or ESMAtlas API), AD isoform pLDDT, top STRING combined score (÷10 for display; raw scale 0–1000), AD-specific exon mean phyloP100way, CT-specific exon mean phyloP100way. Genes are stratified by evidence tier: Tier A (red, KIF21B/PTPRF/FANCA — high-confidence, corroborated by ≥2 independent modules), Tier B (orange, NDUFS4/DLG1/IFT122/SYNE1/RGS3 — moderate evidence), Tier C (grey, ADGRB2 — marginal; M11/M12 evidence divergence). Cell type of isoform switch origin shown as colour chip (Exc, Excitatory; Inh, Inhibitory; OPC, oligodendrocyte precursor; Ast, Astrocyte). FANCA AD phyloP = −0.493 is the only negative value (accelerated evolution) in the panel, highlighted in red. N/A entries denote absence of isoform-specific exons on the indicated side.

**(B)** Per-residue ESMAtlas pLDDT for KIF21B CT kinesin motor domain fragment (aa 1–380, red) and AD WD40 propeller core (aa 370–620, blue), derived from segmental ESMFold predictions (≤400 aa per query). Dashed horizontal lines indicate mean pLDDT for each fragment (CT = 93.2; AD = 94.6). Both folds are independently well-ordered (pLDDT > 70 for >95% of residues), confirming that the KIF21B isoform switch represents a gain-of-fold rather than a loss-of-structure event. Shaded regions indicate the primary domain extent for each isoform.

**(C)** Domain-level mean pLDDT for PTPRF isoforms, extracted from the canonical AlphaFold structure (UniProt P10586). CT isoform domains (red bars) include phosphatase catalytic domains (Y_Phosphatase = 72.3; DSPc = 78.6) absent from the AD isoform; AD isoform domains (blue bars) comprise exclusively extracellular Ig-fold modules (Ig_3 = 84.8; I-set = 85.0; V-set = 86.7; C2-set = 87.5). The lower pLDDT of CT phosphatase domains versus AD Ig domains reflects intrinsic structural properties of each fold class, not a quality difference between isoforms; both are AlphaFold-confident (>70). The absence of PTP catalytic domains in the AD isoform is the primary functional distinction.

## Supplementary Note S-RGS3 — Experimental basis for the RGS3–EFNB2 PDZ interaction and its predicted loss in Alzheimer's disease

The interaction between the PDZ domain of RGS3 (contained in the brain-enriched CT isoform, aa 188–260) and the C-terminal −YYKV carboxylate-binding motif of EFNB2 was established experimentally by Lu et al. (2001, *Cell* 105:69–79; PMID 11301003) using yeast two-hybrid screening with the EFNB2 intracellular domain as bait against a mouse embryonic cDNA library, with hits confirmed by co-immunoprecipitation from neural tissue lysates and extended by functional epistasis assays demonstrating that PDZ-domain integrity is required for ephrin-B reverse signaling and inhibition of G-protein-coupled chemoattraction. The PDZ domain of RGS3 is both necessary and sufficient for binding the −YYKV motif (Lu et al. 2001); the interaction was subsequently extended to all three ephrin-B subtypes (EFNB1, EFNB2, EFNB3) and shown to regulate cortical neural progenitor balance (Kim et al. 2008, *J Cell Biol* 181:973–983; PMID 18541704). The in vivo necessity of the EFNB2 −YYKV motif is independently established by the severe lymphatic vascular malformation phenotype of ephrinB2ΔV/ΔV knock-in mice (Makinen et al. 2005, *Genes Dev* 19:397–410; PMID 15687262), and multiple additional PDZ-domain proteins bind the same motif at synaptic ephrin-B complexes: PICK1, Syntenin-1, GRIP1 (PDZ 6-7), Dvl2, and Par3 (Makinen et al. 2005; Torres et al. 1998; reviewed in Kullander & Klein 2002). RGS3 expression in astrocytes is confirmed by adenosine receptor regulation studies (Patel et al. 2015, *PLoS ONE*; PMC4532427) and injury-response transcriptomics (PMC5658970), providing cell-type context for the AD astrocyte isoform switch.

In Alzheimer's disease, BISECT predicts that the dominant RGS3 transcript shifts from the CT isoform (PDZ×3 + RGS; STRING EFNB2=996, top partner) to the short AD isoform retaining only the RGS GAP domain (aa 394–509), which entirely lacks PDZ sequence and therefore has no ephrin-B2 docking capacity. This mechanistically clean domain loss would abolish tonic G-protein regulation at astrocytic EFNB2 signaling hubs. The astrocytic ephrin/Eph axis is an active AD-relevant pathway: astrocytic EphA4 upregulation promotes synapse elimination in AD models (PNAS 2025, doi:10.1073/pnas.2420324122). Disruption of RGS3-mediated Gαi attenuation at EFNB2 reverse-signaling complexes would be predicted to dysregulate astrocyte-neuron communication and synaptic pruning in a cell-type-specific manner.

**Assessment**: The RGS3 PDZ–EFNB2 interaction is **experimentally validated** at the domain and motif level (not computationally predicted); the predicted loss upon CT→AD isoform switch is mechanistically straightforward. Direct co-IP confirmation in adult human brain or AD astrocytes specifically has not been published as of this writing.

**Key citations**: PMID 11301003, PMID 18541704, PMID 15687262, PMID 15488174, PMC4532427, doi:10.1073/pnas.2420324122.

---

## Discussion (Draft) — Functional Identity Reprogramming via Isoform Switching in AD

The nine isoform switches characterized by BISECT M10–M12 collectively reveal a mechanistic principle that extends beyond the individual case: Alzheimer's disease neurons and glia do not simply downregulate genes or alter protein abundance — they systematically replace canonical protein isoforms with structurally distinct variants that retain the same gene locus but encode functionally orthogonal molecular identities.

The three strongest candidates (KIF21B, PTPRF, FANCA) each exemplify a different mode of this functional identity reprogramming. *KIF21B* undergoes the most dramatic architectural transformation: ESMFold structural prediction demonstrates that the CT kinesin motor (pLDDT = 93.2, P-loop ATPase fold) is replaced in AD excitatory neurons by a WD40 β-propeller scaffold (pLDDT = 94.6) that retains no catalytic motor activity but gains protein-protein interaction capacity with the APC/C ubiquitin ligase (ANAPC4_WD40) and nuclear pore complex (Nup160) machinery. This transition — from a microtubule-directed cargo transporter to a ubiquitination scaffold — represents a gain-of-function isoform switch confirmed by both ESMFold structural evidence and STRING experimental PPI support (TRIM3=765, SMO=694). The extreme evolutionary conservation of both exon sets (phyloP AD=4.067, CT=3.842) argues that both isoform architectures have been independently maintained across vertebrates as regulated, biologically distinct states.

*PTPRF* exemplifies a precision loss-of-function switch operating at the domain level. Although global pLDDT is identical (82.0) for both isoforms sharing UniProt P10586, domain-level pLDDT extraction from the canonical AlphaFold model reveals that the CT isoform retains structured phosphatase catalytic domains (Y_phosphatase pLDDT = 72.3; DSPc = 78.6) entirely absent from the AD isoform, which contains only extracellular Ig domains (pLDDT = 84–87). The AD isoform therefore encodes a membrane-anchored or secreted fragment lacking phosphatase activity, capable of binding Liprin-α (PPFIA1=997, PPFIA3=996) and β-catenin (CTNNB1=982) but unable to dephosphorylate them. The greater evolutionary conservation of CT PTP-encoding exons (phyloP=4.341) versus AD Ig exons (2.835) further supports the ancestral role of the phosphatase-active isoform and the derived, potentially pathological, nature of the AD-associated switch. This dominant-negative mechanism — decoupled scaffolding without catalytic output — may contribute to the E/I imbalance characteristic of AD inhibitory neurons through impaired GABAergic synaptic organization.

*FANCA* provides the dataset's most unambiguous loss-of-function signal. The AD-associated isoform (297 aa) loses the Fanconi_A domain required for core complex scaffolding, and the replaced sequence carries a single exon with phyloP = −0.493 — the only negative conservation score in the entire panel, indicating accelerated evolution inconsistent with functional constraint. The thirty-three CT-specific exons encoding the Fanconi_A domain are conserved (mean phyloP = 1.321), and STRING confirms that the CT isoform engages the entire Fanconi Anemia repair complex (FANCF/C/E/G/L/M=999, BRCA1=995, UBE2T=998). This isoform switch represents selective suppression of the DNA interstrand crosslink repair pathway in AD excitatory neurons — a cell type with high transcriptional activity and consequent R-loop accumulation — through splicing rather than transcriptional silencing.

The downstream consequences of Fanconi Anemia pathway disruption in post-mitotic neurons are mechanistically connected to core AD pathological features through the DNA damage response. When interstrand crosslink repair fails, stalled replication forks in mitotically quiescent neurons are replaced by transcriptional stalling events: RNA polymerase II pausing at template lesions generates persistent R-loops (RNA:DNA hybrids) at highly transcribed neuronal loci, including *MAPT* itself, which contains G-quadruplex-forming sequences prone to R-loop stabilization (Groh et al. 2017, *Nat Commun*). R-loop accumulation activates the ATM/ATR kinase cascade in a replication-independent manner (Walker et al. 2021, *Nucleic Acids Res*), and sustained ATM signalling in neurons promotes CDK5 hyperactivation through p35→p25 calpain cleavage, directly driving tau hyperphosphorylation at epitopes characteristic of neurofibrillary tangle formation (S202/T205, S396/S404; Li et al. 2017, *Sci Rep*). Independently, nuclear TDP-43 — which functions in R-loop resolution through its RNA-binding domains — undergoes cytoplasmic mislocalization when R-loop burdens exceed the nuclear resolution capacity (Jiang et al. 2022, *Nat Cell Biol*), recapitulating the TDP-43 proteinopathy observed in 30–50% of AD cases. Thus, the FANCA isoform switch places a single splicing event upstream of both tau hyperphosphorylation and TDP-43 mislocalization in AD excitatory neurons, connecting pre-mRNA processing dysfunction to the canonical hallmarks of neurodegeneration through a mechanistic pathway that is experimentally tractable and therapeutically targetable.

Among Tier B candidates, *SYNE1* and *RGS3* are notable for the precision with which their domain-loss events connect to AD-relevant cell-biological processes. In AD inhibitory neurons, the SYNE1 isoform switch removes Spectrin repeat domains that anchor the LINC complex to the actin cytoskeleton, severing the nuclear–cytoskeletal mechanical coupling mediated by SUN1/SUN2 (STRING = 999) and the nuclear lamina (LMNA = 996; LMNB1 = 998). In post-mitotic neurons, LINC complex integrity is required for nuclear positioning, chromatin organisation, and nucleocytoplasmic transport of tau-regulatory factors; its disruption in AD inhibitory neurons, which provide the primary GABAergic counterbalance to excitatory hyperactivation, is consistent with the progressive E/I imbalance and nuclear envelope dysfunction documented in AD neuropathology. In AD astrocytes, the *RGS3* switch from the PDZ-scaffolded CT isoform to the GAP-only AD isoform spatially liberates Gαi-inhibitory activity from the EFNB2 reverse-signalling complex established by Lu et al. (2001, *Cell*; PMID 11301003). Because EphB/EFNB2 reverse signalling in astrocytes gates complement-mediated synaptic pruning, decoupling RGS3 from EFNB2 would be predicted to destabilise the spatial precision of Gαi quenching at synaptic contacts, potentially promoting the aberrant complement-driven synapse elimination that is a well-documented early feature of AD pathophysiology.

Beyond these Tier A candidates, the expanded nine-case panel demonstrates that BISECT M10–M12 provides calibrated, not uniformly positive, evidence. The concordance between M11 PPI support and M12 conservation defined two interpretive patterns: candidates where both modules are aligned (KIF21B, PTPRF, FANCA, SYNE1, RGS3) versus candidates where M11 nominally supports but M12 flags low conservation of the gained sequence (ADGRB2, phyloP=0.075). The ADGRB2 case — where STRING returns a coherent nuclear transport protein cluster (RANBP2/RANGAP1/XPOT/KPNB1) despite low HRM exon conservation — illustrates the framework's built-in disambiguation capacity: multi-evidence divergence is itself an interpretable signal that guides experimental prioritization toward Tier A candidates and away from marginal associations.

The PTPRF hypothesis revision (SLIT2 decoy receptor → Liprin-α dominant-negative, driven by STRING falsification of the initial prediction) demonstrates that M11 functions as an empirical falsification tool rather than a confirmatory annotation layer. Taken together, BISECT's M10–M12 framework converts statistical isoform switch prioritization into a multi-evidence hierarchy in which structural confidence, network biology, and evolutionary constraint each contribute independent information — enabling the identification of candidates with the highest probability of experimental validation and therapeutic relevance in Alzheimer's disease.
